sections.title: The Principle of Antagonism
points: Rule: Muscles can only PULL; they cannot push a bone back into its original position.
To allow back-and-forth movement, muscles work in Antagonistic Pairs.
Definition: Two muscles that work in opposition—when one contracts to move a bone, the other must relax to allow the movement.

sections.title: The Thigh Team: Quads & Hamstrings
points: Quadriceps (Front): The Extensor. They contract to straighten the leg (e.g., when kicking a football).
Hamstrings (Back): The Flexor. They contract to bend the leg at the knee (e.g., when lifting your foot while walking).
These muscles are attached to the Femur (origin) and the Tibia/Fibula (insertion).

sections.title: Physical Changes
sections.text: During Contraction, a muscle becomes Shorter, Thicker, and Harder. During Relaxation, it becomes Longer, Thinner, and Softer.

sections.title: Exam Logic: Lifting a Load
points: When you lift a heavy bucket, your Biceps contract to provide the force.
The Tendons (which are non-elastic) transmit this pull directly to the bone.
This process requires a lot of energy from Glucose and Oxygen.

cards.fact: A pair of muscles where one contracts while the other relaxes.
cards.mnemonic: Think of a Tug-of-War between two muscles!

cards.fact: Flexor bends the joint; Extensor straightens it.
The end of the muscle attached to the bone that moves.
